Necrosis of leaflets and deterioration and decay of root system are other salient features.
The leaflets curve inwardly to produce ribbing so that the whole frond develops a cup-like appearance.
Abnormal shedding of buttons and immature nuts are also noticed.
management: Cut and remove disease-advanced, uneconomical palms yielding less than 10 nuts per palm per year.
Grow green manure crops (cowpea, sunhemp, Mimosa invisa, etc.) in coconut basins during April-May and incorporate during September-October.
Irrigate coconut palms with at least 250 litres of water per week.
Adopt suitable inter/mixed cropping in coconut gardens.
Provide adequate drainage facilities.
Apply 50 kg FYM or green manure and 5 kg of neem cake per palm per year.
Apply fertilizers at recommended rates; supply magnesium @ 500g MgO per palm per year.
To manage insect vectors, treat the top two leaf axils with phorate 10G mixed with sand or powdered neem cake.

name: Leaf Rot Disease
scientific_name: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, Exserohilum rostratum and Fusarium spp.
symptoms: The first symptom is the appearance of water-soaked brown lesions in the spear leaves of root-wilt affected palms.
Gradually these spots enlarge and coalesce resulting in extensive rotting.
As the leaf unfurls the rotten portions of the lamina dry and get blown off in wind, giving a fan shape to the leaves.
Sometimes the symptom becomes very acute and the spear fails to unfurl.
management: Remove the rotten portions from the spear and the two adjacent leaves.
Pour fungicide solution of Hexaconazole 2ml or Mancozeb 3g in 300ml water per palm to the base of spindle leaf; 2-3 rounds of spraying are sufficient in case of mild infection.
Spray the crowns and leaves with 1% Bordeaux mixture or 0.5% copper oxychloride or 0.4% mancozeb in January, April-May and September.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: This disease occurs in the nursery.
It is recognized by regular or irregular brown to black leaf blotches surrounded by yellow haloes, which develop along the margin, centre or tip of the leaves.
It causes heavy seedling loss.
management: The disease can be controlled by spraying Mancozeb or Captan at the rate of 200g/100 litres of water.
Copper fungicides should not be used because of the extreme susceptibility of oil palm seedlings to copper burn.

name: Foot Rot / Anabe Roga
symptoms: The leaflets in the outer whorls of leaves become yellow and spreads to the whole leaf and the leaves droop down covering the stem.
Later, the inner whorl leaves also become yellow; subsequently all the leaves droop, dry up and fall off, leaving the stem alone.
The stem also becomes brittle and easily broken by heavy wind.
The base of the stem shows brown discolouration and oozing of dark fluid.
Bracket-shaped fructifications of the fungus called anabe appear at the base of the trunk.
Roots become discoloured, brittle and dried.
When infected trunk is cut, brown discolouration can be seen up to one metre from ground level.
management: Clean cultivation.
Destruction of infected trees and stumps.
Maintaining optimal plant population without overcrowding.
Providing good drainage facility, fertilizers and manures.
Digging trenches to avoid root contact from diseased to healthy trees.
Drench with 1% Bordeaux mixture at frequent intervals.

name: Yellow Leaf Disease
symptoms: Yellowing of tips of leaflets in 2 or 3 leaves of the outermost whorl.
Brown necrotic streaks run parallel to veins in unfolded leaves.
The yellowing extends to the middle of the lamina; tips of the chlorotic leaves dry up.
In advanced stage all the leaves become yellow.
Finally the crown leaves fall off leaving a bare trunk.
Root tips turn black and gradually rot.
management: Maintain trees in seed vigour.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Application of potassium and magnesium more than the recommended level.

name: Mahali / Kolerogo / Fruit Rot
symptoms: Rotting and excessive shedding of immature nuts from the trees.
Water soaked spots initially develop at the base of the nut; fruit stalks and rachis of inflorescence are also affected.
Nuts show large vacuoles and dark brown radial strands.
Very often the top of the affected trees dries up resulting in withering of leaves and bunches.
Affected nuts fall off and show the white mycelial growth of the fungus.
management: Clean cultivation.
Destruction of affected trees.
Collection and burning of fallen nuts.
Spray with Bordeaux mixture 1% before onset of the monsoon and second spray 40-45 days later.

name: Bud Rot
symptoms: The first symptom is the change of spindle leaf colour from green to yellow and then brown.
The leaves rot and the growing bud rots causing death of the palm.
The affected young leaf whorl can be easily pulled off.
The outer leaves also become yellow and droop off one by one leaving a bare stem.
management: Destruction and removal of dead palms and bunches affected by mahali disease.
Drenching crowns with Bordeaux mixture 1% including surrounding healthy palms.

name: Inflorescence Die-Back and Button Shedding
symptoms: Disease appears on rachillae of the male flowers and then in the main rachis as brownish patches which soon spread from tip downwards covering the entire rachis causing wilting.
The female flowers of the infected rachis shed and the whole inflorescence shows die-back symptom.
The fruiting bodies of the fungus (conidia) appear as concentric rings in the discoloured areas.
The disease is severe mostly during dry conditions (February-March).
management: Spraying with COC 0.25% or Mancozeb 0.2%.

name: Algal Leaf Spot
symptoms: Leaves develop lesions that are roughly circular, raised and purple to reddish-brown.
The alga produces microscopic, rust-coloured, spore-like bodies on the surface of the leaf spots, giving them a reddish tinge.
The alga may spread from leaves to branches and fruit.
Most algal spots develop on the upper leaf surface.
Older infections become greenish-gray and look like lichen.
management: Avoid plant stress.
Avoid poorly drained sites.
Promote good air circulation in the plant canopy to reduce humidity and duration of leaf wetness.
Removal of infected portions by spraying of Bordeaux mixture.
Destruction of affected plant portions.
Improving the nutrient status of the soil by application of nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium.

name: Brown Blight / Grey Blight
symptoms: Small, oval, pale yellow-green spots first appear on young leaves; often the spots are surrounded by a narrow, yellow zone.
As the spots grow and turn brown or gray, concentric rings with scattered, tiny black dots become visible.
Eventually the dried tissue falls, leading to defoliation.
Leaves of any age can be affected.
management: Avoid plant stress.
Grow tea bushes with adequate spacing to permit air to circulate and reduce humidity and the duration of leaf wetness.
Spray Copper Oxychloride or Bordeaux mixture 0.1% during winter season and summer season.

name: Blister Blight
symptoms: Small, pinhole-size spots are initially seen on young leaves less than a month old.
As the leaves develop, the spots become transparent, larger and light brown.
After about 7 days, the lower leaf surface develops blister-like symptoms with dark green, water-soaked zones surrounding the blisters.
Following release of the fungal spores, the blister becomes white and velvety.
Subsequently the blister turns brown, and young infected stems become bent and distorted and may break off or die.
management: Removal of affected leaves and shoots by pruning and destruction of the same.
Spraying of Bordeaux mixture or Copper Oxychloride 0.1%.
A mixture of 210g of Copper oxychloride + 210g of nickel chloride per ha sprayed at 5 days interval from June to September and October to November.
Spray Tridemorph at 340 and 560 ml/ha under mild and moderate rainfall conditions.

name: Horse Hair Blight
symptoms: Black fungal threads resembling horse hair are attached to upper branches and twigs by small brown discs.
The fungus penetrates and infects the twigs from the discs and produces volatile substances that cause rapid leaf drop.
management: Remove and destroy all crop debris from around plants.
Prune out infected or dead branches from the plant canopy.

name: Twig Dieback / Stem Canker
symptoms: The first symptoms include browning and drooping of affected leaves.
As the disease spreads into the shoots, they become dry and die.
The entire branch can die from the tip downward.
Dying branches often have cankers: shallow, slowly spreading lesions surrounded by a thick area of bark.
management: Plant in well-draining, acidic soils.
Remove diseased twigs by cutting several inches below cankered areas and disinfecting them.
Spray appropriate protective fungicides during periods of wet weather or natural leaf drop to protect leaf scars from infection.

name: Black Root Rot
symptoms: The fungus originates from the dead heaped leaves 5-7.5 cm above the soil level; from there it spreads to the roots region of tea bushes.
When bark is removed, star-like growth of mycelium can be seen.
At the surface of the soil the mycelium surrounds the stem and kills the bark for a length of 7.5-10.0 cm.
A swollen ring of tissue is formed round the stem above the dead patch.
management: Removal and destruction of infected plant.
Clean cultivation without fallen leaves.
Dig a trench around the infected bush to provide sunlight which prevents the spread of mycelium.

name: Rust
symptoms: It mostly affects leaves and sometimes tender shoots; in earlier stage, 2-3 mm size yellow spots develop on the lower surface of leaves.
Rust lesions develop as orange yellow spots on lower surface of leaves.
The upper surface of the leaves become yellow to brown.
At later stages, yellow rusty appearances are found on the entire undersurface of the leaf; orange yellow spots are followed by black spots and necrosis.
Infected leaves defoliate and branches remain without any leaves.
The plants remain stunted with reduced yield.
management: Collection and destruction of diseased leaves.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Spraying of Bordeaux mixture 0.5% before flowering, during rainy and after rainy seasons in the months of May, August-September and October respectively.

name: Brown Leaf Spot
symptoms: Brown spots with grey centre and brown margin surrounded by yellow hallow are developed.
The lesion spreads to major leaf area.
On berries dark blotches are developed; berries shrink and drop.
management: Spraying with Bordeaux mixture 0.5%.

name: Damping Off / Collar Rot
symptoms: It causes pre-emergence damping off and post-emergence damping off.
In post-emergence damping off the collar region near soil level is infected leading to rotting of tissue and death of seedlings.
management: Soil drenching with Copper oxychloride 0.25%.

name: Die-Back / Anthracnose
symptoms: On leaves, circular to grayish spots of 2-3 mm in diameter.
On berries small dark coloured sunken spots are formed; beans become brown.
Die-back also occurs.
management: Spraying Mancozeb 0.25%.

name: Black Rot / Koleroga
symptoms: The most important symptom is dark brown or black decaying leaves, twigs and berries.
The leaves hang down by strong fungal strands; mycelial threads can be seen on twigs and petioles.
Sclerotia can be seen all over the affected areas.
Defoliation and fruit drop occurs.
management: Removal of diseased portions.
Spraying with Bordeaux mixture 1%.

name: Root Rot
symptoms: Stump or brown root-rots: affected bushes show a gradual yellowing of leaves and defoliation and death; affected roots are brittle and show dark brown wavy lines of the fungus.
Fusarial root rot or wilt: a sudden wilting and yellowing of leaves leading to defoliation and death of aerial parts; roots turn brown to pinkish.
management: Burn the plants at site and isolate affected patches by digging deep trenches.
Remove the affected plants and treat the soil at 1kg lime per plant to raise the soil pH.
Treat the soil around the affected area with PDCB or brassicol at 0.4%.
Maintain the vigour of plants.

name: Berry Blotch
symptoms: Necrotic spots on the exposed surface of green berries enlarge and cover the major portion.
Fruit skin shrivels and sticks fast.
management: Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture during June and late August.
Maintain medium shade overhead.

name: Abnormal Leaf Fall
scientific_name: Phytophthora palmivora and P. nicotianae var. parasitica
symptoms: It occurs during June-August, while general leaf fall occurs during December.
On leaves dull grey, circular spots appear which enlarge and become irregular.
The petiole exhibits sunken spots; affected fruits rot.
Leaves shed prematurely either green or after turning coppery red.
The affected leaves form a thick carpet of rotting foliage which emits bad smell.
management: Prophylactic sprays prior to onset of south west monsoon with Bordeaux mixture 1%; addition of ZnSO4 @ 0.2% improves the efficacy.

name: Birds Eye Spot
symptoms: Circular spots with grey centre and with brown border occur in large numbers in nursery or on young plants in the field.
management: Spray with 1% Bordeaux mixture.

name: Corynespora Leaf Spot
symptoms: Circular spots with brown or papery centre and with a dark brown ring appear on the young leaves.
A yellow halo develops around the ring.
Shot holes occur; affected leaves dry up.
management: Spray with 1% Bordeaux mixture.

name: Pink Disease
symptoms: Prevalent in wet areas; young twigs and branches are mostly affected.
The fungal growth encircles the stem, penetrates the bark and cortical tissues which eventually decay.
The bark splits and peels off.
If the infection is not recognized early, the tree dies after the rainy season.
management: Affected parts should be pasted with Bordeaux paste or painting tar.
Copper fungicides should not be used in rubber because they will contaminate the latex.

name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: White powdery fungal growth appears on young leaves and also on matured leaves.
Infected leaves curl, crinkle, roll inwards and fall off leaving the petiole attached to the tree giving a broom stick appearance.
The infected flowers and tender fruits shed.
management: Spray sulphur 3-5 times at 15 days interval.
Carbendazim 0.1% or Tridemorph 1.5% dusting alternated with sulphur dust 70%.
Carbendazim + wettable sulphur and microsul (52% EC) is also effective.

name: Brown Root Disease
symptoms: The affected roots are encrusted with mass of soil and small stones held by network of mycelium.
The affected tap root becomes rotted and the entire tree is killed.
management: Removal and destruction of the infected trees.
Apply lime @ 2.5 t/ha.
In the area of dead tree apply additional dose of lime @ 25 kg per dead tree.
Wash the partially affected root with Emisan or Aretan 0.1%.

name: Colletotrichum Leaf Disease
symptoms: Infects tender leaves, mostly at the leaf tip region.
Spots are small, brown in colour and are surrounded by a yellow halo.
Numerous spots coalesce and dry up leading to defoliation.
The infected leaves often crinkle and become distorted before shedding.
management: Spraying with Bordeaux mixture 1%, copper oxychloride 0.125%, mancozeb 0.2% or carbendazim 0.05% at 10-15 days intervals is effective.

name: Seedling Blight
symptoms: The symptoms develop on the leaves and stem of seedlings or budded plants.
On leaves, small water soaked lesions appear which later coalesce in the blighting of leaves.
On stem, water soaked lesions develop initially and later turn to black colour.
Stem infections develop at any point on the stem causing the death of seedlings.
management: Remove and destroy the affected seedlings.
Spray with 1% Bordeaux mixture or 0.2% copper oxychloride just before the onset of monsoon and thereafter at frequent intervals.

name: Black Pod Rot
scientific_name: Phytophthora palmivora, P. megakarya, P. citrophthora and P. capsici
symptoms: Infection appears as chocolate brown spot, which spreads rapidly and soon occupies the entire surface of the pod.
As the disease advances, a whitish growth of fungus consisting of fungal sporangia is produced over the affected pod surface.
Ultimately, the affected pods turn brown to black.
The internal tissues as well as the beans become discoloured as a result of infection.
management: Periodically remove the infected pods.
Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture with onset of monsoon and also at frequent intervals.
Provide frequent drainage and regulate shade to increase aeration.
Pseudomonas fluorescens (Pf1) liquid formulations @ 0.5% as soil and foliar spray 3 times per year (June, October and February) was found to be effective in reducing cocoa pod rot and stem canker.

name: Stem Canker
symptoms: The cankers appear either on the main trunk, jorquettes or fan branches.
The earliest symptom is the appearance of a greyish brown water soaked lesion on the outer bark.
A reddish brown liquid oozes out from these lesions, which later dries up to form rusty deposits.
The tissues beneath the outer lesion show reddish brown discolouration due to rotting.
management: It can be controlled in the initial stages by the excision of diseased bark followed by wound dressing with Bordeaux mixture or copper oxychloride paste.
Wilted branches should be cut and removed.

name: Vascular Streak Dieback (VSD)
symptoms: The first indication of the disease is a characteristic yellowing of one or two leaves on the second or third flush behind the growing tip.
Diseased leaves fall within a few days of turning yellow and the other leaves on the shoot show similar symptoms.
When the infected shoot is split lengthwise there is always a characteristic brown streaking.
management: The disease can be controlled by disposing diseased branches and regular pruning of chupons on the trunk.
Cocoa nurseries should not be located near the diseased area.
Avoid getting seedlings from diseased tracts.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.

name: Cherelle Rot
symptoms: The shriveling and mummifying of some young fruits are a familiar sight in all cocoa gardens.
In the early stages the fruits lose their lustre and in four to seven days they shrivel.
The fruits may wilt but do not abscise.
Many other factors like insects, diseases, nutrient competition and over production may also be associated with this problem.
management: Remove and destroy shriveled and mummified fruits from the trees.
Maintain proper tree nutrition and balanced fertilization.
Spray with 1% Bordeaux mixture at the onset of infection.
Ensure proper canopy management to reduce humidity and improve aeration.
Monitor and control insect pests that may predispose fruits to fungal infection.

name: Black Spot
symptoms: Black lesions with feathery margins surrounded by yellow tissue are found on the leaves.
Infected leaves drop prematurely.
Purple or red bumpy areas on first year canes may be evident.
Plants may be weakened due to defoliation and reduced flower production may be observed.
management: Roses should be planted where the sun can quickly dry the night's dew.
Space roses far enough apart for good air circulation.
Avoid overhead watering and keep foliage as dry as possible.
Remove infected canes and burn diseased leaves.
Spray with Mancozeb 0.25% or Chlorothalonil 0.2% at 10-14 day intervals during humid conditions.

name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: The symptom appears as grayish-white powdery substance on the surfaces of young leaves, shoots and buds.
Infected leaves may be distorted, and some leaf drop may occur.
Flower buds may fail to open, and those that do may produce poor-quality flowers.
It is most severe in shady areas and during cooler periods.
management: Collection and burning of fallen leaves.
Spray with Wettable sulphur 0.3% or Carbendazim 0.1%; 2-3 sprays at 15 days interval is effective.
Sulphur dust at 25 kg/ha.
Use of sulphur at higher temperature conditions will be phytotoxic.

name: Die Back
symptoms: Drying of twigs from tip downwards.
Blackening of the twigs.
The disease spreads to root and causes complete killing of the plants.
management: Pruning should be done so that lesions on the young shoots will be eliminated.
Apply chaubatia paste in the pruned area.
Spray Carbendazim 0.1% or Copper oxychloride 0.25% on the pruned area to prevent re-infection.
Collect and destroy all pruned infected material.

name: Rust
symptoms: Lemon yellow pustules appear on lower surface of the leaves and stems.
Then the colour changes to blackish red.
The affected leaves turn yellow, become deformed and fall prematurely.
Die-back symptom also appears due to weakening of the plant.
management: Collection and burning of fallen leaves.
Spray with Wettable sulphur 0.25%.

name: Fusarium Wilt
symptoms: In young plants, the first sign of the disease is fading or graying of the normal colour of the leaves with wilting of the leaves and young stems.
It is followed by eventual collapse of the whole plant.
When older plants are infected, similar symptoms are produced but the older leaves may show chlorosis followed by an indistinct purple-red discoloration.
The vascular tissues of infected stems is stained dark brown.
Mature plants show wilt symptoms over a period of several months before they die and eventually become straw coloured.
management: The diseased plants should be removed immediately after noticing the disease.
Complete root system and surrounding soil should be dug out and disposed off carefully.
Soil solarization using clear transparent polyethylene film (0.1 mm thick) for 30 days gives satisfactory control.
Treat soil with Carbendazim 0.1% drench before planting.
Use disease-free planting material from certified sources.

name: Alternaria Leaf Spot
symptoms: The chief symptom is blight or rot at leaf bases and around nodes, which are girdled.
Spots on leaves are ashy white.
The centre of old spots are covered with dark brown to black fungal growth.
Leaves may be constricted and twisted and the tip may be killed.
Branches die-back at the girdled area and black crusts of conidia are formed on the cankers.
management: To reduce the disease incidence, humidity may be kept low by providing proper air circulation.
Disease-free planting material should be used.
Planting material should be dipped in Bacillus subtilis 2g/litre of water.
Spray Mancozeb 0.25% or Chlorothalonil 0.2% at 10-15 day intervals on noticing initial symptoms.

name: Bacterial Wilt
symptoms: The upper parts of established plants turn pale and wilt.
The stem develops elongated discolored stripes and splits open, which is characteristic of the disease.
The roots are rotted partially and the cortical tissues become sticky and show discoloration.
The leaves are twisted; the roots are generally lacking on one side and remain discolored.
The base of the cutting is discolored, with an elongated brown area extending upwards.
management: Use cuttings taken from upper parts of the healthy stock plants as they are less liable for infection.
Diseased plant debris should be collected and burnt.
Overhead watering and splash watering should be avoided.
Disease-free planting materials are to be used.
Use region-specific or locally adapted less susceptible varieties.

name: Wilt
symptoms: Initial symptoms are in the form of yellowing and browning of leaves.
Affected leaves die from the base of the plant upward.
Infected plants are stunted and often fail to produce flowers.
Wilting may cause rotting of root or the base of the stem.
management: Before planting, dip the rooted cuttings in a solution of Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 2g/litre of water.
Since the disease spreads mostly through cuttings, it is important to use disease-free planting material.
Disease can further be minimized by following strict sanitation, periodical monitoring, crop rotation and roguing of infected plants.

name: Rust
symptoms: The disease symptoms are in the form of brown blister-like swellings, which appear on the undersides of leaves.
These burst open releasing masses of brown, powdery spores.
Severely infected plants become very weak and fail to bloom properly.
management: Early removal of infected leaves and plants helps to prevent the further spread of the disease.
Spray Wettable sulphur 0.3% or Mancozeb 0.25% at 10-15 day intervals on noticing symptoms.
Collect and destroy all infected plant debris.

name: Septoria Leaf Spot
symptoms: Leaf spots occur during cool-wet periods of the rainy season.
Since the pathogens are spread through rain splashes the lowermost leaves get infected first.
Serious infection may result in premature withering of the leaves; the dead leaves hang to the stem for some time.
When flowering starts the infection occurs on flower buds, which rots completely.
management: Destruction of disease debris and avoiding excessive irrigation is recommended.
Spray Mancozeb 0.25% or Chlorothalonil 0.2% at first sign of disease.
Avoid overhead watering to reduce leaf wetness.

name: Powdery Mildew
symptoms: Infection is more severe in older plants under humid conditions.
The growth of the fungus on the leaves appears as powdery coating.
Infected leaves turn yellow and dry out.
Infected plants remain stunted and fail to flower.
management: Good ventilation and proper spacing for free circulation of air is recommended.
Spray Wettable sulphur 0.3% or Carbendazim 0.1% at 10-15 day intervals.
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves.

name: Cercospora Leaf Spot
symptoms: Circular to irregular reddish brown spots of 2-8 mm diameter appear on the upper surface of the leaves.
Later the spots become irregular covering larger areas of the leaves.
management: Spraying with Carbendazim 0.1%.

name: Alternaria Leaf Blight
symptoms: In the leaves dark brown spots appear.
Under humid conditions the spots enlarge covering larger areas causing blighting of leaves.
Concentric rings can be seen on the lesions.
The disease also affects stem, petiole and flowers.
management: Collection and removal of fallen leaves.
Spray with Mancozeb 0.25%.

name: Collar Rot and Root Rot
symptoms: Plants at all stages are infected.
First the older leaves become yellow followed by younger leaves and finally death of the plant.
In the root black discolouration can be seen.
On the infected tissues and stem surface white strands of mycelia and mustard-like sclerotia are seen.
management: Heavy application of FYM with Trichoderma viride.
Soil drenching with Carbendazim 0.1% or Copper oxychloride 0.25% around the base of the plant.
Remove and destroy affected plant parts.
Improve soil drainage to prevent waterlogging.

name: Phyllody
symptoms: Leaves become small, malformed and bushy.
In the place of flowers, green leaf-like malformed flowers are formed.
management: Selection of cuttings from healthy plants.
Spraying insecticide to control the vector.
Rogue out and destroy affected plants.
Control leafhopper vectors with systemic insecticides such as Imidacloprid 0.3 ml/l.

name: Root Rot
symptoms: The infection results in stunted growth.
Ultimately the entire plant dries.
Rhizoctonia solani causes more losses and can attack older plants.
management: Soil sterilization controls the disease.
Drenching with Bacillus subtilis 2g/litre of water.
Ensure proper drainage and avoid waterlogging.
Treat soil with Carbendazim 0.1% drench before planting.

name: Powdery Mildew
scientific_name: Erysiphe cichoracearum and Oidium erysiphoides f.sp. gerbera
symptoms: The fungus forms white powdery coating on the foliage.
management: Spraying with 0.2% Wettable sulphur controls the disease.
Diseased leaves should be removed and destroyed.
Provide adequate spacing for good air circulation.
Spray Carbendazim 0.1% if wettable sulphur is not effective.

name: Blossom Blight
symptoms: The disease appears as light brown, irregular, water soaked spots on flower stalks and petals.
The spots increase rapidly and coalesce with one another and form distinct depressed lesions.
Under humid conditions the infections become severe involving the entire flower head and resulting in blossom blight and stalk rot.
The disease is favoured by drizzling rains and cool moist weather.
The fungus is soil-borne and the infection starts from the base touching the soil.
management: Use of disease-free soil for cultivation reduces the disease incidence.
Affected flowers should be collected and destroyed.
Excessive watering should be avoided.
Drench with Metalaxyl 0.1% or Copper oxychloride 0.25% around the base of the plant.
Improve drainage to prevent waterlogging.

name: Stem Rot
symptoms: The disease symptoms are preceded by the appearance of prominent spots of loose green colour due to rotting which extend and cover the entire leaf.
The infected leaves get detached from the plant.
More or less round sclerotic, brown spots are formed on and around the infected leaf.
As a result, the infected plant becomes weak and unproductive.
management: Drenching with Pseudomonas fluorescens 2g/litre of water.
Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4g/kg of soil at the base of the plant.
Remove and destroy infected plant material.
Improve drainage and avoid excessive moisture at the base of the plant.

name: Root Rot
symptoms: Discolouration and rotting of roots are the prominent symptoms.
management: Dipping stem cuttings in Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 2g/1 litre of water for 20 minutes followed by drenching with P. fluorescens @ 2g/1 litre on 30 days after planting.
Ensure well-drained soil to prevent waterlogging.
Apply Trichoderma viride @ 2.5 kg/ha with FYM before planting.
Avoid excess irrigation.

name: Leaf Blight
symptoms: Initially, small circular brown spots appear on the leaves.
Later the spots enlarge with characteristic concentric rings and leads to blighting of entire leaves.
management: Spray Bacillus subtilis 2g/l of water on 30 and 60 days after planting.
Remove and destroy infected leaves.
Spray Mancozeb 0.25% or Chlorothalonil 0.2% at 10-15 day intervals on noticing symptoms.

name: Root Rot and Tuber Rot
symptoms: Yellowing of leaves, discolouration and rotting of roots and dark brown lesions on the stem are the prominent symptoms.
Rotting of tubers also occurs.
The presence of sclerotial bodies as small, black dot-like structures are seen in the stem portions.
management: Tuber treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 2g/1 litre of water for 20 minutes.
Drenching with Bacillus subtilis 2g/l of water on 30 days after planting.
Use disease-free planting material.
Improve soil drainage and practice crop rotation.

name: Leaf Blight
symptoms: Alternaria leaf spot is characterized by small spots which are initially water-soaked.
These spots turn reddish-brown, may reach 1/8 inch in diameter and are roughly circular.
management: Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 10g/kg of seed.
Spray Mancozeb 0.25% or Chlorothalonil 0.2% at 10-15 day intervals on noticing symptoms.
Remove and destroy infected plant debris.
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops.

name: Blue Mould
symptoms: It occurs on fruits during storage and transit.
Affected rind of the fruits becomes watery.
Watery spot increases and then entire fruit rots and emits bad smell.
Blue fungal growth is seen on the surface of the fruits.
management: Treat with Benomyl or Thiophanate-methyl.
2% bleaching powder dip for 5 minutes or 0.2% flit 406 for 10 minutes.
TBZ 0.1% incorporated in waxol under refrigeration.
Pre-storage dip in TBZ (500 ppm) for 2-3 minutes.

name: Alternaria Rot
symptoms: Typical rot symptoms are nearly round, brown to black lesions, often centred around a skin break or weakened tissue.
The spots are firm, dry and shallow.
The surface of spots becomes dark brown to black.
In the advanced stages, the rotted tissues become spongy and the affected flesh turns black.
management: Practice orchard sanitation and soft handling of fruit.
Harvest fruit at proper maturity.
Careful handling during picking, washing, and packing.
Store fruits at temperatures of 0 to 4 degrees C.

name: Green Mould Rot
symptoms: It occurs on fruits during storage and transit.
Affected fruit becomes watery.
Watery spot increases and then entire fruit rots and emits bad smell.
Green fungal growth is seen on the surface of the fruits.
Spores of the pathogen are air-borne.
Entry through the stylar-end or lenticels.
management: Treat with Benomyl or Thiophanate-methyl.
2% bleaching powder dip for 5 minutes or 0.2% flit 406 for 10 minutes.
Storage at 2-4 degrees C.
TBZ 0.1% incorporated in waxol under refrigeration.
Pre-storage dip in TBZ (500 ppm) for 2-3 minutes.
Boric acid at 4% and Imazalil.

name: Apple Scab
symptoms: Small, rough, black, circular lesions on the skin.
Fruits after keeping in cold storage show symptoms.
Affected fruits rot due to secondary infection of the lesions.
Low temperatures of 4 and 8 degrees C favour the development of disease.
management: Infected spurs and cankers should be pruned during summer.
Affected fruits whether on trees or fallen on the ground should be collected and buried in the soil.
Diseased mummies should be ploughed into the soil.
Store fruits at low temperature with proper ventilation.

name: Grey Mould
symptoms: Infected fruits turn slightly brownish.
The fungus advances into the inner flesh resulting in a soft, watery mass of decayed tissue contained in a slightly intact, brown skin.
The pathogen sporulates on the surface of fruit in the typical, powdery, grey mould stage.
The disease can spread by contact.
management: Sodium bisulphate which releases SO2 when in contact with moist air can be used with packing material.
Grey mould can be controlled by prompt cooling.
Maintain good sanitation in storage areas.
Remove and destroy infected fruits promptly.

name: Black Rot
symptoms: Rot first appears on the calyx end of the fruit as a firm, black, metallic-like spot with concentric rings.
Typical decay spots are circular and medium brown.
Reddish border and appear anywhere on the apple surface.
management: Treatment of fruits with linseed oil or mustard oil or castor oil.
Remove all dead wood and infected fruit from storage.
Maintain low temperature storage to slow decay development.

name: Bitter Rot
symptoms: Small, brown, circular spots present.
Later become sunken, forming a saucer-shaped depression.
In wet weather conditions, pink fruiting bodies of the fungus develop in the center of the rotten area.
Rot penetrates deeply into the flesh.
management: Spray Mancozeb 0.25% in field.
Treatment with Mancozeb 0.25% to check the disease in storage.
Remove infected fruits from storage to prevent spread.

name: White Rot
symptoms: One type rots the fruit from the inside outward; entire apple becomes soft but retains its shape and takes on a light brown color.
Second type presents as small, brown, circular spots on the fruit similar to bitter rot; spots are softer than black rot and more cup-shaped than bitter rot.
management: Eliminate dead wood including current season's fire blighted twigs.
Avoid stub cuts; make pruning cuts properly just outside of the bark ridge and collar tissue.
Maintain proper storage sanitation and remove infected produce.

name: Brown Rot
symptoms: Enlarged rots are soft but not mushy.
Circular and medium brown during the early and medium stages of development.
Decayed area enlarges, small black spots about 1/8 inch across gradually develop at the lenticels.
Entire fruit is decayed and under warm conditions turns black and develops a velvety sheen.
In warm, moist conditions gray to tan fungal tufts develop on the decayed surface.
management: Control of brown rot begins with sanitation.
All twigs with brown rot cankers need to be pruned and removed from the area or destroyed.
Store fruits at low temperatures to slow disease progression.
Remove and discard infected fruit immediately.

name: Blister Spot
symptoms: Raised blisters that develop on fruit lenticels from early to the middle of July.
The blisters become purplish-black and range in diameter from 1 to 5 mm at harvest.
management: Treat with Benomyl or Thiophanate-methyl.
2% bleaching powder dip for 5 minutes or 0.2% flit 406 for 10 minutes.
Storage at 2-4 degrees C.
Pre-storage dip in TBZ (500 ppm) for 2-3 minutes.
Handle fruit carefully to minimize injuries that encourage bacterial infection.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Small, black, circular specks on the skin that are sunken and coalesce to form large spots.
Bright salmon-coloured conidial mass appears on the spots.
Severely infected fruits become dark due to blemishes.
Acervuli develop on the skin and the pulp becomes partially soft.
Non-latent infection usually starts during or after the harvest of bunches in small peel wounds.
Many latent infections at the time of harvest show large number of appressoria on the surface of the peel.
The spread of the disease is by air-borne conidia and numerous insects.
management: Post-harvest dipping of fruits in Carbendazim 400 ppm, or Benomyl 1000 ppm, or Aureofungisal 100 ppm.
Avoid damage to fruits at harvest and during transit.
Store bananas under controlled temperature and humidity conditions.

name: Crown Rot
scientific_name: Fusarium roseum, Lasiodiplodia theobromae, Deightoniella torulosa
symptoms: Darkening of the hand and the adjacent peduncle.
The discoloured area covers almost one fourth of the fruit if conditions are favourable.
Loss of ability of hand to support fruit.
Occurrence of black tip due to fruit piercing moth.
Wind-blown bunches develop severe spotting on the fingers during rainy weather.
management: Control of crown rot starts in the field with the regular removal of leaf trash.
Proper field sanitation can greatly reduce the number of crown rot fungi spores present.
Do not keep rotting fruits or plant waste materials near the packing station.
Maintain clean washing water in the delatexing baths and change the water frequently.
Dehanding should be done carefully with a sharp knife to avoid leaving a ragged cut.
Post-harvest treatment of fruits with an effective fungicide is essential.

name: Cigar-End Rot
symptoms: Rot begins at the tip of immature fruit and spreads upward.
Ashy conidia and conidiophores cover the rotted portion.
Imparting a burnt, ashy cigar-end appearance with a dark border.
Decay may extend up to one-third of the fruit but internal tissues develop a dry rot.
management: The principal method of control is frequent manual removal and burning of dead flower parts and infected fruits.
Use of fungicide to control the disease is also recommended.
In the packhouse, care should be taken to cull infected fruits to avoid contaminating the washing water with spores.
Cigar-end rot is effectively controlled by covering the flower immediately after emergence with a polyethylene bag before the hands emerge.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Superficial leathery appearance.
Silver or grey to dark lesions.
Tear-staining pattern common.
Pink tinge from spores under humid conditions.
Infection occurs by rain-splash during autumn.
Ethylene degreening increases sensitivity to anthracnose.
management: Dead wood should be pruned as the fungus harbours in dead branches.
Field sprays of copper-based fungicides should be applied prior to autumn rains.
Postharvest treatment with Benzimidazole fungicides may reduce fruit losses.

name: Septoria Spot
symptoms: Dark brown collapsed lesions with a purple tinge.
Black specks develop in decayed area.
Infection occurs in autumn but remains dormant until cool conditions.
Fruit is more susceptible after frosts.
management: Field application of copper-based fungicides.
Careful handling during harvest to avoid injuries that may accelerate post-dormancy decay.
Store citrus fruits under appropriate cool temperature conditions.

name: Blue and Green Mould
scientific_name: Penicillium digitatum (green mould) and Penicillium italicum (blue mould)
symptoms: Softening of damaged tissue.
White fungal growth which progressively turns blue or green as spores develop.
Postharvest fungicides can arrest spore development resulting in white only fungal growth.
Infections develop from damaged areas.
Late season fruit is more susceptible.
Damaged rind is more susceptible.
management: Careful handling reduces damage to rind.
Good hygiene and sorting reduces spore load and infection rates.
Sanitation destroys spores in recirculating water and packingline equipment.
Postharvest fungicides such as Imazalil should be applied within 24 hours of harvest.
Lower storage temperatures slow down fungal development.

name: Sour Rot
symptoms: Very soft, watery decay.
Distinct margin between decayed and healthy tissue.
Sour odour detectable.
Infection occurs in damaged fruit.
Sour rot spores in soil can accumulate in recirculating water in dips and drenches.
Spreads by contact after packing creating nests of infected fruit in boxes.
management: Careful handling reduces rind damage.
Apply Guazatine fungicide within 24 hours of harvest.
Strong emphasis on sanitisers when Guazatine fungicide is not approved for use.
Use sanitized wash water and packing equipment.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: The most characteristic symptoms appear as small pin-head sized spots on the unripe fruits.
They gradually enlarge to form sunken and circular, dark brown to black spots.
The infected area of the unripe fruits becomes harder and corky.
Acervuli are formed on fruit stalks.
The pathogen remains dormant for about three months in the young infected fruits.
In moist weather, acervuli appear as black dots scattered throughout the dead parts of the twigs.
The conidia are spread by wind or rain.
management: Spraying the trees with Bordeaux mixture 0.6% or Copper oxychloride 0.2% before the onset of monsoon reduces the disease incidence.
Careful handling during harvest and transit to avoid injuries.
Store fruits at cool temperatures to slow decay progression.

name: Phomopsis Fruit Rot
symptoms: The symptoms appear on unripe fruits at the blossom-end.
Infected fruits show small, conspicuous, white or light brown and circular spots.
As the fruits ripen, the spots extend and cover the fruit surface.
The infected tissues become softer and emit an undesirable odour.
management: Spray Bordeaux mixture 0.4%, Copper oxychloride 0.2%, or Zineb 0.2%.
Harvest fruits at proper maturity and avoid injuries.
Remove infected fruits promptly from storage to prevent spread.

name: Botryodiplodia Rot
symptoms: It causes considerable damage during storage and transit.
The affected fruits show brownish discolouration mostly at the stem-end which gradually proceeds downwards in an irregular wavy manner.
In advanced stages, numerous small pycnidia are produced over the entire surface of the fruit.
The rot is soft and watery.
Maximum decay occurs at 30 degrees C.
management: Careful handling during harvest and transit.
Storage at 15 degrees C and quick disposal can minimize losses.
Avoid mechanical injuries to fruits during harvest.

name: Fruit Canker
symptoms: Minute, brown or rust-coloured unbroken, circular, necrotic areas appear on the fruit which later tear the epidermis open in a circinate manner.
The margin of the affected area becomes raised.
management: Spray 1% Bordeaux mixture or lime-sulphur (1 in 25) 3 or 4 times.
Post-harvest wash with aureofungin (200 ppm) can protect fruits for 5 days.
The fruit rot can be checked by dip treatment of the fruits with aretan.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Infection occurs on leaves, stems, young flowers and fruit.
Sunken black spots appear on the surface of the fruit during ripening.
Infection of fruit is usually latent and manifests itself only as the mango begins to ripen.
The disease is most severe in wet weather.
The latent infection is carried from the field to storage.
management: Spray with mancozeb (2 g/L) weekly during flowering and then monthly until harvest; stop spraying 14 days before harvest.
Dip fruit within 24 hours of harvest by totally submerging them for 5 minutes in hot water (52 degrees C) with carbendazim at 100 mL per 100 L water.
Temperature must be carefully controlled to within 0.5 degrees C to prevent fruit damage.
As an alternative, apply prochloraz 45% at 55 mL/100 L of water at ambient temperature as a non-recirculated spray.
Postharvest treatments will not provide complete disease control; field management is also important.

name: Stem End Rot
symptoms: In fruits, the pericarp darkens near the base of the pedicel.
The affected area enlarges to form a circular, black patch which under humid atmosphere extends rapidly and turns the whole fruit completely black within two or three days.
The pulp becomes brown and softer.
management: Avoid harvesting immature fruit.
Postharvest treatment with hot water and carbendazim is partially effective against stem end rot.
For control during controlled atmosphere storage, a dual treatment of hot carbendazim followed by prochloraz is necessary.
Fruit from orchards with a history of stem end rot losses should be rejected for long term storage.

name: Diplodia Stem End Rot
symptoms: Epicarp darkens around the base of the pedicel.
Circular, black patch forms under humid atmosphere.
Soft rot aided by pectinolytic and cellulolytic enzymes.
More portions of fruit turn black and soften.
Rapid loss of ascorbic acid and non-reducing sugars.
management: Avoid harvesting immature fruit.
Handle fruit carefully to avoid injuries that serve as entry points.
Post-harvest hot water treatment combined with fungicide dip reduces incidence.
Store mango fruits at appropriate low temperature and humidity.
Remove and destroy infected plant parts from the orchard to reduce inoculum.

name: Black Mould Rot
symptoms: Yellowing of base with development of irregular, hazy, greyish spots.
Mesocarp of the rotted area becomes depressed and soft.
The fruit surface becomes covered with blackish fungal growth.
Decrease in ascorbic acid.
management: Handle fruit carefully to avoid wounding which promotes infection.
Store mango at appropriate low temperature to slow fungal development.
Maintain sanitation of storage and packing facilities.
Post-harvest fungicide dips with appropriate registered products.

name: Brown Spot
symptoms: The affected area of fruits becomes olivaceous-black and shrinks.
Black dots appear at the centre of the spots representing the acervuli.
On matured green fruits, small brown spots appear with greyish white centre which later turn to bigger lesions with large number of acervuli seen as black dots.
Wounding leads to more disease incidence.
management: Handle fruits carefully to avoid wounds during harvest and transit.
Store fruits at cool temperatures to slow disease development.
Remove and destroy infected plant debris in the orchard.
Post-harvest dip in appropriate fungicide solution.

name: Black Soft Rot
symptoms: Discrete and discoloured areas all over the fruit.
Turn dark brown to black at maturity.
Black fruiting bodies appear on the spots.
Pathogen is soil-borne; conidia transmitted by wind and rain water.
management: Avoid injuries to fruits during harvest and packing.
Post-harvest dip treatment with appropriate fungicide.
Store mango at low temperature and appropriate humidity.
Sanitation of harvesting and packing equipment.

name: Soft Rot
symptoms: The disease appears as water-soaked spots covering the entire fruit within 3 to 4 days.
Rotted fruits become soft and dark brown.
Later numerous acervuli are seen in rotted zones.
The fungal colonies are yellowish white.
management: Spray Mancozeb 0.25%.
Handle fruits carefully to minimize wounding.
Store fruits at cool temperatures to slow decay.
Remove infected fruits promptly to prevent spread to healthy fruits.

name: Fruit Rots
scientific_name: Phytophthora palmivora, Pestalotiopsis versicolor, Pestalotiopsis glandicola, Pestalotiopsis sapotae and Botryodiplodia theobromae
symptoms: Diseased fruits exhibit water-soaked lesions which become brown within 2 to 3 days.
Subsequently the whole fruit is covered with tufts of mycelium.
management: Spray Mancozeb 0.25% as a pre-harvest preventive measure.
Harvest fruits at proper maturity to reduce susceptibility.
Store at cool temperature to reduce disease progression.
Discard infected fruits promptly and sanitize storage containers.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: The spots on fruits first appear as brown superficial discolouration of the skin which develop into circular, slightly sunken areas 1 to 3 cm in diameter.
Gradually the lesions coalesce and sparse mycelial growth appears on the margins of the spots.
Under humid conditions, an encrustation of salmon pink spores are released.
Infection at early stages of fruit results in mummification and deformation.
management: Foliar spraying with carbendazim 0.1% at 45 days interval.
Chlorothalonil 0.2% at 10 to 15 days interval.
Thiophanate-methyl 0.1% or Mancozeb 0.2% at 10 days interval controls the disease.
Fumigation with benzylisothiocyanate controls post-harvest spots and rots.

name: Rhizopus Rot
symptoms: Irregular, water-soaked lesions which gradually enlarge and get covered by white and dark brown fungal growth and sporangiophores.
The fruits become watery and emit a foul odour.
Invasion occurs through wounds.
The rot rapidly destroys the entire fruit and quickly spreads to other fruits.
Fruit fly punctures after harvest increase the disease incidence.
management: The disease can be checked by sorting the fruit at 10 degrees C or below.
The disease can be controlled with DCNA dips at 1000-2000 ppm.
Hot water treatment of fruits at 49 degrees C for 20 minutes can check the rot.
Avoid injuries to fruit during harvest and packing.

name: Fruit Rot
symptoms: It is characterized by grey-brown, circular to semi-circular patches covered with mycelium and conidiophores.
management: Handle fruits carefully to avoid injuries during harvest and transport.
Store fruits at cool temperatures to slow disease development.
Post-harvest dip with appropriate fungicide such as Mancozeb or Carbendazim.
Remove and discard infected fruits immediately from storage.

name: Phomopsis Fruit Rot
symptoms: Affected fruit develops a water-soaked spot which increases in size.
The whole area becomes soft and pulpy.
The rotten area turns dark brown to black and gets depressed and cracks at a later stage.
Water-soaking is seen in the area surrounding the diseased portion.
All stages of fruit are infected by the pathogen.
management: Borax, benlate, captan, difolatan and dithane M-45 are effective.
Handle fruits carefully to minimize wounding.
Store at cool temperature to reduce disease progression.

name: Stem End Rot
symptoms: It incites a stem end rot and a surface fruit rot.
It usually induces a wider and softer water-soaked margin and greater internal discolouration.
Sporulating lesions of B. theobromae are black and have a rough surface caused by the erumpent, confluent arrangement of pycnidia.
The disease development is more rapid on ripe and half-ripe fruits.
The rot begins as dark green, water-soaked spots.
Later, the affected portion becomes shriveled and turns dark brown.
The infection of fruit stalk results in fruit drop.
management: Hot water treatment at 49 degrees C for 20 minutes.
Handle fruits carefully to avoid stem injuries during harvest.
Remove and discard infected fruits promptly from storage.

name: Alternaria Rot
symptoms: Dark grey, slightly sunken irregular lesion, developing lengthwise.
Grey cottony mycelial mat bearing conidia appeared on the surface of developing lesions.
When diseased pod is cut transversally, blackened necrotic tissues are visible.
management: Spray 0.2% Zineb or 0.2% Mancozeb.
Handle pods carefully to avoid wounds during harvest.
Store at cool, dry conditions to slow disease development.

name: Rhizoctonia Rot
symptoms: Pod turns greenish colour turning brown, and the infected tissues become fully covered with mycelia.
Internally, immature seeds and placenta were infected and the diseased tissues were light brown to black.
Externally, mycelia tend to be fluffy and lighter in color, forming a large number of dark sclerotia on the fruit surface.
management: Plant debris from previous crops should be removed before planting.
Quintozene sprayed in the soil before sowing gives effective control.
Harvest pods at proper maturity and handle carefully.
Store at cool, dry conditions to minimize post-harvest losses.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Sunken lesions on fruits vary in size.
Up to 1.3 cm wide and may coalesce.
Tan colored ooze of fungal spores appear on lesions.
Fruit dries and becomes black leading to fruit drop.
management: Field sanitation: destruction of infected plant debris.
Harvest fruits before they over ripen.
Spray Zineb or Ferbam at 10 days interval.
Handle fruits carefully during harvest and transit to prevent wounding.

name: Fruit Rot
symptoms: Fruits are soft, watery and decay.
Finally fruits turn black and mummified as pycnidia develop abundantly over the surface.
Stem lesion is dark brown, becoming grey in the centre as pycnidia develop.
Leaf shows irregular brown spots.
management: Seed treatment with hot water at 50 degrees C for 30 minutes.
Spray 0.2% Difolatan or Captan 0.2% at weekly interval in nursery.
Field spray with Zineb 0.2%, Bordeaux mixture 0.8%.
Remove and destroy infected fruits from storage promptly.

name: Phytophthora Fruit Rot
symptoms: Fruits develop small, water soaked lesions.
Skin shows discolouration, turns greyish brown, and develops white cottony mycelium in humid weather.
Rotten parts become depressed and develop wrinkles.
Rotting spreads deep into the pulp and turns brown, watery and soft.
Rapidly spreads during rainy season.
management: Removal and destruction of the affected fruits.
Spraying the crop with Difolatan (0.3%) thrice at an interval of 10 days.
Avoid injuries to fruit during harvest and storage.
Maintain cool and dry storage conditions.

name: Sour Rot
symptoms: Soft, watery, colourless decay on carrot roots.
Decayed area is covered with dull, white spores of the pathogen and a vinegar-like odour may develop.
Fungus is a soil inhabitant that infects carrots through wounds.
In storage, warm temperature and improper ventilation favour the disease.
management: Good field drainage.
Minimizing wounding of carrots.
Good sanitation using new or disinfected storage containers.
Pre-cooling and storing carrots at 0 degrees C is essential.

name: Crown Rot
symptoms: Damping-off of carrot seedlings and a crown rot later and during storage.
Field symptoms include premature senescence and death of foliage.
On carrot roots, dark brown sunken lesions or cankers near the crown or in other parts of the root.
management: Cultural practices that reduce crown injury and enhance soil drainage and air circulation within the canopy are recommended.
Plant debris from previous crops should be removed before planting carrots.
Carrots should not follow perennial crops such as alfalfa.
Quintozene sprayed in the soil before sowing gives effective control.

name: Cottony Soft Rot / White Mould
symptoms: Foliage develops water soaked, dark olive-green lesions associated with collapsed tissues.
Lesions expand rapidly over the entire leaf, petiole, and rosette.
Infected tissues are covered by abundant cottony white mycelium.
At an advanced stage, affected tissues exhibit a bleached appearance.
Large black sclerotia (2 to 20 mm) form externally embedded in the mycelium or internally within the pith of the petiole.
management: Good ventilation in storage.
Use of clean containers.
Maintenance of temperature near 0 degrees C.

name: Phytophthora Root Rot / Rubbery Brown Rot
symptoms: Infected portions of the root become dark brown to black, water-soaked and rubbery in consistency.
Lesions may occur in one or more bands anywhere on the carrot root.
White mycelia may be present on lesions, which facilitates the spread of the fungus to adjacent roots.
management: Maintaining adequate soil drainage.
Avoid prolonged periods of water saturation.
Storage conditions: temperature at 0 degrees C and RH less than 95%.

name: Root Dieback
symptoms: Root dieback of carrots produces excessively branched or stubbed roots.
Fungus kills young tap roots after seed germination, reducing root length and stimulating forking.
Forking and stubbing occur, but these symptoms can also be caused by soil compaction and nematodes.
management: Avoiding excessive watering.
Providing good field drainage.
Planting carrots in deep, friable and well-drained soils.
Post-emergence fungicides such as Mefenoxam to control damping-off.

name: Bacterial Soft Rot
symptoms: Cells become water soaked, middle lamella is destroyed and the cells collapse.
Soft, watery or slimy consistency.
Rotted tissues are grey to brown, accompanied by foul odour.
In the field, tops of rotted carrots turn yellow and wilt as roots break down.
management: Careful handling at harvest to minimize bruising of carrot roots.
Washing and dipping in a solution of sodium hypochlorite (5.25%).
Storage at temperature just above freezing (0 degrees C) and RH 90%.
Crop rotation with alfalfa, beans, beets, and corn.

name: Crater Rot
symptoms: Band of dark brown necrosis around the crown and horizontal brown canker-like lesions mostly on the crown and upper roots.
Small pits subsequently develop beneath the lesions that enlarge into sunken brown crater lines with white flocculent mycelium produced under high humidity.
management: Rapid removal of field heat.
Application of Dowicide (O-phenylphenol) at 98 g/L.
8-year rotation with non-host crop.
Using disease-free seed.
Bacillus subtilis and T. viride soil application.

name: Bacterial Soft Rot
symptoms: Dark veinal tissue followed by leaf chlorosis and necrosis.
Internal dark brown discolouration.
Stem cankers develop causing breakage of branches.
Fruit peduncle is highly susceptible and is frequently the initial point of infection.
Both ripe and green fruit may be affected.
Initially, lesions on the fruit are light to dark-colored, water-soaked and somewhat sunken.
In later stages, bacterial ooze may develop and affected fruit hangs from the plant like a water-filled bag.
management: Early detection of symptoms.
Disinfection of pruning tools.
Avoidance of wounding plants.
Remove plant debris such as fallen and diseased leaves.
Use chlorinated water to reduce populations of soft rot bacteria during washing.
Allow fruit to dry thoroughly.
During packing and storage, keep fruit clean and maintained in a cool, dry place.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Ripe fruits turning red are affected.
Small, black, circular spot appears on the fruit skin.
Badly diseased fruits turn straw colour or pale white colour and lose their pungency.
Diseased cut open fruits show the lower surface of the skin covered with minute, elevated sclerotia.
In advanced stage, seeds are covered by a mat of fungal hyphae and turn rusty in colour.
management: Use disease-free seeds.
Seed treatment with Thiram 2 kg/ha or Zineb 2.5 kg/ha.
Three sprayings with Captan 0.2%: first spraying just before flowering, second at fruit formation, and third a fortnight after second spraying.
Biocontrol: Pseudomonas fluorescens and Bacillus subtilis are effective.

name: Gray Mould
symptoms: Brownish spots develop near the soil line or cotyledons.
Water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems darken and collapse.
Water-soaked spots rapidly expand into large yellowish-green or grayish-brown, irregular lesions that are soft and spongy in texture.
Velvet-like fungus mycelium and spores are produced on the lesion surface under cool, humid conditions.
management: Field sanitation: remove and burn decaying infected plant parts.
Space seedlings and transplants to allow for free flow of air through the crop.
Treatment with hot air at 38 degrees C for 48-72 hours.
Hot water at 50 to 53 degrees C for 2 to 3 minutes.

name: Late Blight
symptoms: Infected leaf tissue wilts, becomes light green or gray-green, later becoming tan to white and scalded in appearance.
With moisture, leaf spots have a water soaked border.
Fruit rots are irregular in shape and olive green or light green with water soaked borders.
Rots expand rapidly and fruits can be completely diseased and desiccated, forming mummified fruits.
Infected seeds are brown and shriveled.
management: Rotation with non-susceptible crops will reduce the amount of Phytophthora capsici surviving in soil.
Fresh, clean seeds should be planted in new potting mix to establish healthy transplants.
Monitor seedlings as well as the field and remove diseased plants as soon as they occur.
Avoid storing infected fruits and discard mummified fruits.

name: Anthracnose
symptoms: Older leaves show small, water-soaked or yellowish areas that enlarge rapidly and turn tan to reddish brown.
Spots are often circular to angular.
Petioles and stems develop tan to black elongated slightly sunken streaks called cankers.
Immature fruit turns black, shrivels and dies.
Round, water-soaked spots develop on older fruit, turn dark green to brown with age and may become sunken.
Under wet conditions, pinkish-colored spore masses ooze out of the sunken spots.
management: Field sanitation: destroy the plant debris.
Hot water treatment of seeds at 57.2 degrees C for 20 minutes.
Seed treatment with thiram, carbendazim, or mancozeb at 2 g/kg.
Spraying at weekly intervals of Carbendazim 0.1%, Mancozeb 0.2%, Difolatan 0.2%.
Fruit dip for 5 minutes in wash water containing 120 ppm of chlorine helps prevent infection of healthy fruits.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.

name: Gummy Stem Blight and Black Spot
symptoms: Leaf develops water-soaked lesion with interveinal necrotic scorch.
Lesions are surrounded by a yellow halo; when spots dry up, they often crack.
Stem lesions are water-soaked and later appear tan; often cause gummy, reddish-brown or black beads to exude.
Affected area becomes brownish and water soaked.
In advanced stages, rind becomes black and deeply wrinkled.
Large irregular areas of the fruit become bronzed with distinct concentric rings.
management: Disease-free seed.
Two-year crop rotation out of all cucurbits.
Field sanitation.
Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil, mancozeb, and benomyl.
Pre-cool cucumbers to 10 degrees C or lower temperature.

name: Choanephora Wet Rot
symptoms: Attacks the blossoms first and progresses into the developing fruit causing a wet rot at the blossom end.
Fruit rot progresses rapidly and can affect entire fruit within one or two days.
Sporulation by the fungus appears as spines with dark heads on the surface of infected tissues.
management: Reduce soil moisture using raised beds.
Prevent fruit injury.
Prevent soil contact with fruit using plastic mulches or trellising.
Harvest fruits at proper stage of maturity.
Minimize cucurbit fruit injuries at harvest.
Pre-cool fruit and maintain relatively low storage temperature.

name: Pythium Fruit Rot
symptoms: Fruits in intimate contact with soil are affected.
Forms a luxuriant woolly mycelial mat on the affected fruits.
Skin of the fruit shows soft, dark green, water soaked lesions.
Interior tissue becomes watery and soft and decaying matter emits a bad odour.
Spreads among the fruits during storage and transit.
management: Soil drenching with copper oxychloride 0.25%.
Fruits should be kept away from soil.
Store fruits at cool, dry conditions to minimize losses.

name: Belly Rot
symptoms: Dark brown water-soaked decay on the side of the fruit in contact with the soil.
Followed by a yellowish-brown discolouration of the fruit surface.
Entire fruit rots within a few days.
management: Pre-harvest sprays of fungicides including Azoxystrobin, Chlorothalonil, or Thiophanate-methyl.
Holding the fruit at 10 degrees C will retard disease development during transit and storage.
Prevent fruit contact with soil using mulches or trellising.

name: Diplodia Fruit Rot
symptoms: Stems and leaves show blight and wilting.
Fruit decay appears around the stem.
Rind becomes slightly darkened, water soaked and light brown later.
Centre of the spots turns black, cracks and wrinkles.
management: Scratches and bruises must be avoided.
Pre-cooling after harvest.
Harvest fruits with long stems.
Cut ends should be painted with a fungicide paste such as copper sulphate.

name: Bacterial Soft Rot
symptoms: Infects the fruit via cracks or wounds in the skin.
Soft rot rapidly disintegrates the flesh, turning it into a soft mass of leaky tissue.
Infected fruits typically have a foul odour.
management: Avoid injury to the skin during harvest and handling.
Use properly sanitized wash water (150 ppm hypochlorous acid).
Store at cool, dry conditions and discard infected fruits promptly.

name: Phytophthora Fruit Rot
symptoms: Lesions appear on fruit surface.
Fruit rot develops on the side contacting the soil.
Fruit rot can result from an infected leaf falling on the fruit.
Severely infected fruits are collapsed.
management: Rotation with non-host crops is recommended.
Manage soil moisture by selecting well-drained fields, avoiding low-lying areas, subsoiling, and preparing dome-shaped raised beds.
Do not over-irrigate.
Prevent movement of contaminated soil on equipment between fields.

name: White Rot
symptoms: Leaves decay at the base, turn yellow, wilt and topple over.
Roots and bulbs are covered with a fluffy white mycelium.
Affected bulbs may become watery and the outer scales crack as the bulb dries and shrinks.
Small black sclerotia form on and in affected bulb parts.
management: Rotating out of Allium crops for ten years.
Destroy infected tissue.
Plant disease-free seed stock.
Seed dressing with benomyl or carbendazim (100-150 g/kg of seed).

name: Basal Rot
symptoms: Plants show reduced emergence, yellowing and browning (necrosis) of leaves beginning at tips.
Reduced bulb size, bulb decay and brown, poorly developed root systems.
In storage, bulbs show spongy, sunken, yellow brown rotting lesions.
In early stages, infected bulbs are softened, brown and watery when cut open.
White, light pink or reddish fungal growth covering the cloves or in rot cavities.
Deep cracks form in the cloves, followed by break down of tissue.
management: Avoid rotations with Allium spp. such as onions and leeks, and cereals.
Store bulbs at cool temperatures and low humidity with good ventilation.
Avoid storing damaged bulbs.

name: Blue Mould Rot
symptoms: A blue-green powdery mould is observed on cloves in soil and in storage.
Air-borne spores spread the disease.
Infection first occurs on wounds sustained when cloves are separated from the parent bulb.
management: Bulbs are harvested carefully to avoid wounds and bruising.
Promptly dry or cure bulbs after harvest.
Store at cool temperature with good ventilation to prevent mould development.

name: Black Mould
symptoms: Whole tissue becomes black powdery mass.
Individual bulbs shrivel and become light in weight.
Under high humid conditions the inner tissues become moderately soft.
Infected bulbs lose their pungency and smell.
Rotten garlic cloves show black, brown, pink or white coloured rotting.
management: Rapid and thorough curing after harvest.
Storage with good ventilation.
Store at temperature just above 0 degrees C.

name: Pink Rot
symptoms: Roots are affected and they turn pink or reddish and sometimes darken to a red or purple colour.
Black spores form on the diseased roots which eventually shrivel and die.
management: Long rotations should be practiced to prevent build-up of inoculum in the soil.
Plant disease-free planting material.
Improve soil drainage to reduce infection conditions.

name: Neck Rot
symptoms: Found upon the bulbs at the time of harvest.
Affected scale tissue becomes soft.
Dense layer of grey mould appears at the neck.
Infection progresses most rapidly down the scales which were originally infected.
management: Promoting rapid drying at harvest and good aeration in storage is best for managing Botrytis on bulbs.
Cooler storage temperatures may help control the disease.
Dust seed with benomyl at 1 g/kg of seed before planting.

name: Neck Rot
symptoms: Latent disease although infection takes place in the field.
Softening of scales which take on a water soaked appearance.
Under moist conditions, a greyish sporulating mycelial mat develops on the surface of the scales.
management: Dusting the seed with benomyl at 1 g/kg of seed.
Avoid excessive and late applications of nitrogen.
Rapid and thorough drying directly after harvest.
Provide good ventilation for curing onions before storage.

name: Blue Mould Rot
symptoms: Initial symptoms are water soaked areas on the outer surface of scales.
Later a green to blue green powdery mould may develop on the surface of the lesions.
Infected areas of fleshy scales are tan or grey when cut.
In advanced stages, infected bulbs may disintegrate into a watery rot.
management: Avoid wounds and insect damage to bulbs.
Harvest and handle onion bulbs with a minimum of bruising or wounding.
Cure the bulbs so the necks are dry.
Store bulbs at temperatures of 5 degrees C or less with low relative humidity.

name: White Rot / Mouldy Nose / Dry Rot
symptoms: Leaves turn yellow and dieback.
Roots are rotten and base of the bulb is covered with a watery or grey fungal growth.
Numerous small black spherical sclerotia are produced.
management: Long rotation of 8 to 10 years.
Good drainage.
Use of healthy planting materials.
Destruction of wild onion and leeks.
Seed dressings with benomyl or carbendazim.

name: Bulb Rot / Basal Rot
symptoms: Occur in patches.
Leaves turn yellow and then dry up slowly.
Entire plant shows complete drying of the foliage.
Bulb shows soft rotting and the roots get rotted.
Whitish mould growth on the scale.
management: Field sanitation: destruction of infected plant debris.
Soil drenching with copper oxychloride 0.25%.
Pre-planting treatment of bulbs with benomyl 15% + mancozeb 60%.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.

name: Black Stalk Rot
symptoms: Affects garlic and leek as well as onion.
Attacks only diseased, injured bulbs and aging tissue.
Damages by weakening the seed stalks when they are almost ripe, causing them to break over.
Spoils the appearance of the bulb with a sooty mould before they are marketed.
management: Harvest without causing any bruises.
Bulbs should be dried properly before storage.
Store with good ventilation and at appropriate cool temperature.

name: Purple Blotch / Scald Disease
symptoms: Whitish minute dots on leaves with irregular chlorotic areas on the tip portion.
Circular to oblong concentric black velvety rings appear in the chlorotic area.
Lesions develop towards the base of the leaves.
Leaves die from the tip downwards and break at the point of infection.
Bulbs show semi-watery rot, shrinkage of the fleshy bulb scales and desiccation followed by the scales becoming dry and papery.
management: Seed treatment with thiram at 2.5 g/kg of seed.
Three sprays of Copper oxychloride 0.25%, Chlorothalonil 0.2%, Zineb 0.2%, or Mancozeb 0.2%.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Ensure proper drying and curing of bulbs before storage.

name: Black Mould
symptoms: Masses of black powdery spores on both exterior and between the scales of the bulbs.
Especially along the vascular strands of the veins.
Scales become dry and papery.
management: Rapid and thorough curing after harvest.
Storage with good ventilation.
Store at temperature just above 0 degrees C.

name: Black Leg
symptoms: Aerial stem rot and tuber soft rot.
Black leg begins from a contaminated seed piece.
Stem bases show an inky-black to light-brown decay extending up the stem.
These enlarge into a soft, mushy rot that causes entire stems to wilt and die.
Leaves roll upward at the margins, become yellow, wilt and often die.
Potato tubers with soft rot have tissues that are very soft and watery with a slightly granular consistency.
A black border separates diseased from healthy areas.
In later stages a foul odor and a stringy or slimy decay develops as secondary decay bacteria invade infected tissues.
management: Plant only certified, disease-free seed tubers.
Seed treatment with Streptomycin sulphate 0.1% for 10 minutes or Streptocycline (100 ppm) and copper sulphate (40 ppm) for 30 minutes.
Harvest tubers only after the vines are completely dead to ensure skin maturity.
Take precautions to minimize cuts and bruises when harvesting and handling tubers.
Storage at 55-60 degrees F with 90-95% relative humidity for the first 1-2 weeks to promote wound healing.

name: Dry Rot
symptoms: Dry dark spots appear on the skin which later becomes sunken and wrinkled with irregular concentric rings.
Spots shrink and burst out.
Internal tissue becomes brown and shrunken with cavities filled with numerous white tufts of mycelium.
Rotting progresses into whole tuber which loses much of water and becomes dry, hard, shriveled and light in weight.
management: Avoid injuries to tubers.
Potatoes should be dried thoroughly and then stored in a cool place.
To speed the healing process, hold tubers at 50 to 60 degrees F with good ventilation and RH of at least 95% for the first 2 to 3 weeks of storage.

name: Brown Rot
symptoms: Leaf turns bronze colour, shrivels and dies.
Vascular system of stem, root, stolon and tuber turns brown.
Brown ring appears in the tuber due to discolouration of vascular bundles.
Whitish bacterial exudate oozes from the vascular system of cut stems and cut tubers.
management: Crop rotation of potato with wheat.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Use only certified, disease-free seed tubers.
Avoid storing infected tubers.

name: Late Blight
symptoms: Affects leaves, stems and tubers.
Water soaked spots appear on leaves, turn purple brown and finally black colour.
White growth develops on under surface.
Stem breaks at these points and the plant topples.
In tubers, purplish brown spots spread to entire surface.
Tuber shows rusty brown necrosis spreading from surface to the center.
management: Bruising of tubers at harvest should be avoided.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Regular spraying during growing season at 10 to 15 days interval with Zineb 0.2%, Bordeaux mixture 1.0%, or Mancozeb 2 kg/ha.
Store tubers at low temperature with adequate ventilation.

name: Scab
symptoms: Shallow scab: corky tissue arising from abnormal proliferation of the cells of the periderm of the tuber.
Lesions vary in size and shape and are darker than the healthy skin.
Corky lesions 1 to 3 mm deep and darker than shallow lesions.
Actinomycete attacks young tubers at an early stage of development.
management: Use disease-free planting materials.
Soil application of PCNB (30 kg/ha) at the time of planting.
Green manuring before planting effectively reduces disease incidence.
Seed treatment with mercuric chloride 0.1%.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.

name: Potato Wart
symptoms: Small white granular swellings on the eyes.
Swellings remain minute or may become as large as the tuber.
Soft, pulpy, white to begin and become black later.
management: Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Steam sterilization of soil.
Soil treatment with mercuric chloride and formalin 5%.
Plant only certified, disease-free seed tubers.

name: Sclerotium Rot
symptoms: Thick white strands of fungus appear at the collar region of the stem and roots.
White fungal hyphae grow on the tubers which later start rotting and are covered by fruiting bodies.
management: Application of ammonium nitrate to the soil.
Seed tuber treatment with PCNB at 15 kg/ha.
Use region-specific or locally adapted resistant varieties.
Grow non-susceptible crops like corn and sorghum in rotation.

name: Charcoal Rot
symptoms: Black spots (2-3 mm in diameter) develop around the lenticels which appear as whitish specks at the centre.
On cutting, internal tissues show black patches beneath the spots on the surface of the tuber.
management: Avoid bruising of tubers during harvest, collection and storage.
Temperature of store house should be low.
Use early maturing varieties.
Maintain proper storage conditions with adequate ventilation.

name: Silver Scurf
symptoms: Lesions are brown, slightly depressed and circular with fimbriate margins.
Dotted with minute black specks or sclerotia of the pathogen.
Organism invades only the cork cells which are destroyed and slough off forming a scurf.
Spread from diseased to healthy tubers in storage.
management: Use of disease-free seed material.
Seed treatment with mercuric chloride 0.1% for 30 minutes.
Maintain cool storage conditions.
Handle tubers carefully to avoid injuries.

name: Angular Leaf Spot
symptoms: water-soaked angular spots on leaves
black arm (stem lesions)
boll rot
bacterial ooze
management: Seed treatment with Acid Delinting; spray Streptocycline (100 ppm) + Copper Oxychloride (0.3%).

name: Bacterial Blight (Black Arm)
symptoms: angular leaf spots
blackening of the stem (Black arm)
boll rot with oily appearance
management: Acid delinting of seeds; spray Streptocycline; use resistant cultivars.

name: Grey Mildew (Dahiya)
symptoms: white frosty/powdery growth on leaf underside
angular translucent spots on upper surface
premature defoliation
management: Spray Wettable Sulfur (0.3%) or Carbendazim; destroy plant debris.

name: Leaf Curl Virus (CLCuV)
symptoms: upward or downward curling of leaves
thickening of veins
enations (leaf-like outgrowths) on underside
management: Control Whitefly vector with Imidacloprid; remove alternate weed hosts like Sida acuta.

name: Reniform Nematode
symptoms: stunted plants
smaller bolls
leaves show 'mosaic-like' chlorosis
reduced root system
management: Rotate with corn; use soil fumigants in high-infestation areas.

name: Root Rot (Macrophomina)
symptoms: sudden wilting
shredding of bark at the root-stem junction
darkened taproot
management: Intercrop with Moth bean; apply Zinc Sulphate; avoid moisture stress.

name: Root-Knot Nematode
symptoms: stunting
patchy growth
leaf yellowing
small/fewer bolls
large root galls
management: Use resistant varieties; apply Nematicides; rotate with peanuts (non-host).

name: Tobacco Streak Virus (TSV)
symptoms: necrosis of leaf veins
square/bud drooping
purple-to-brown streaks on stem
management: Remove Parthenium weeds (host for thrips); spray systemic insecticides for thrips control.

name: Grassy Shoot
symptoms: profuse tillering
stunted growth
narrow chlorotic leaves
plant looks like a clump of grass
management: Hot Water Treatment (50°C for 2 hours); rogue out infected clumps; control aphids.

name: Lesion Nematode
symptoms: reddish-brown lesions on roots
poor ratoon growth
thin stalks
general yellowing
management: Apply filter press mud (press mud); use green manure; soil application of Carbofuran.

name: Sett Rot (Pineapple Disease)
symptoms: failure of buds to germinate
internal tissue turns black
distinct pineapple-like odor from fermented sap
management: Drip-treat setts with Carbendazim; avoid waterlogging; ensure optimal soil temperature at planting.

name: Barley Stripe Mosaic
symptoms: yellow-to-white stripes on leaves
v-shaped patterns
shriveled grains
stunted plants
management: Use certified virus-free seeds; eliminate wild grass hosts.

name: Covered Smut
symptoms: grains replaced by black spore mass
spores enclosed by a persistent gray membrane
stunted heads
management: Seed treatment with Vitavax (2.5g/kg); follow proper crop rotation.

name: Loose Smut
symptoms: heads turn into black powdery spore masses
silvery membrane ruptures early
stunted stalks
management: Systemic seed treatment with Carboxin (Vitavax); hot water treatment.

name: Net Blotch
symptoms: criss-cross dark brown lines
net-like pattern on leaves
leaf stripes
management: Seed treatment with Carboxin; use resistant varieties.

name: Scald
symptoms: oval water-soaked lesions
